parents.father_research_notes: John Delafield (1748-1824), English-American merchant and diplomat

notable.notable_blurb: Survived mutiny at sea; chartered NY's first agricultural

narrative_blocks.detail: Continuing north, the ship met a violent storm off France and limped into Bristol, England. There, with Europe in the grip of the Napoleonic Wars and Anglo-American tensions rising toward the War of 1812, Delafield was thrown into prison on an alleged customs violation. He was released but confined within 30 miles of Bristol — 'a stranger without money.' He worked for a cabinet maker and a drug store, married a Bristol woman, and remained under British surveillance until the war's end. When his wife died in 1820 he returned to New York. (Rose Hill Mansion historical article; Willers 1900)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1808, John Delafield was sent as super-cargo on a merchant brig to the West Indies. The captain died of yellow fever in Cuba. The mate died two days after leaving Havana.

narrative_blocks.detail: In 1843 he retired from banking and purchased 352 acres in Seneca County, New York, naming his farm 'Oaklands.' He embraced the new drain tile technology introduced by neighbor John Johnston and became a leading voice for agricultural modernization. His farm was named best in Seneca County (1846) and best in New York State (1848). He was elected president of the Seneca County Agricultural Society and in 1851 became president of the New York State Agricultural Society. (Rose Hill Mansion historical article)
narrative_blocks.body: Back in New York by 1820, Delafield rose from bank teller to president of the Phoenix Bank by 1830. He was an early promoter of the Hudson River Railroad, a director of the University of New York, and helped organize the Philharmonic Musical Society — which became the New York Philharmonic.

narrative_blocks.header: Chartered New York's first state agricultural college
narrative_blocks.detail: He died suddenly on October 22, 1853 — six months after the charter passed. The Syracuse Daily Journal wrote: 'There was probably no man in the State more deeply interested in the progress of Agricultural science and reform than Mr. Delafield, and his labors to urge it forward were arduous and incessant.' The college at Oaklands never advanced after his death. He was survived by his second wife Harriet Wadsworth Tallmadge, daughter of Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge (H00757) — Washington's Culper Ring spymaster. (Willers 1900; Syracuse Daily Journal, Oct 26, 1853)
narrative_blocks.body: On April 15, 1853, the New York State Legislature passed 'An Act to Incorporate The New-York State Agricultural College,' chartered at Delafield's Oaklands farm. It may have been the first state agricultural college in the United States. Delafield was named president of its board of trustees.

burialCemetery.name: Washington Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Burial place of John Delafield III (I00169) and Harriet Wadsworth Tallmadge Delafield (HD1035).

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
